questions.stem: A retail client of Arbour Securities tells her advisor to buy 200 shares 'right away at whatever the market is asking.' Under the UMIR 1.1 definition, this instruction creates a:

options.A: Market order — an order to buy a security to be executed upon entry to a marketplace at the best ask price
options.B: Limit order at the best ask price
options.C: Call Market Order
options.D: Opening Order

questions.rationale: UMIR 1.1 defines a market order as an order to buy a security or a derivative to be executed upon entry to a marketplace at the best ask price (or sell at the best bid price). A limit order (B) requires a client-specified maximum or minimum price, which this client did not give. A Call Market Order (C) trades at particular times established by the marketplace, not on entry. An Opening Order (D) is entered before the opening to execute at the opening price.
questions.source_quote: "“market order” means an order to: (a)     buy a security or a derivative to be executed upon entry to a marketplace at the best ask price" — umir.txt ~line 1381

questions.stem: A trader at Cardingley Capital wants to enter a single order that combines a retail client's buy order with a buy order for the firm's own inventory account. Under UMIR 1.1 definitions, the combined order is a:

options.A: Multiple client order
options.B: Bundled order — an order that includes a client order as well as a non-client order or principal order, or both
options.C: Jitney order
options.D: Program Trade

questions.rationale: UMIR 1.1 defines a bundled order as an order that includes a client order as well as a non-client order or principal order, or both — the inventory side makes this a principal order combined with a client order. A multiple client order (A) is the closest distractor but combines orders from more than one client and expressly excludes principal and non-client orders. A jitney order (C) is entered by one Participant for another Participant. A Program Trade (D) involves market orders in index-component securities tied to an index derivative trade.
questions.source_quote: "“bundled order” means an order that includes a client order as well as a non-client order or principal order, or both." — umir.txt ~line 370

questions.stem: The arbitrage desk at Farrowdale Capital sells an index futures contract and simultaneously enters a series of market orders to buy the particular securities underlying that index, which has been designated by the Market Regulator. Under UMIR 1.1, the resulting trade in the basket of securities is a:

options.A: Special Terms Order
options.B: Program Trade — a trade resulting from a series of market orders for securities underlying a designated index, undertaken in conjunction with a trade in a derivative on that index
options.C: Basis Order
options.D: Contingent Derivative Order

questions.rationale: UMIR 1.1 defines a Program Trade as a trade resulting from a series of market orders for the purchase or sale of particular securities underlying an index designated by a Market Regulator where the trade is undertaken in conjunction with a trade in a derivative the underlying interest of which is the index — the futures-plus-basket structure here. A Basis Order (C) is the closest distractor but is a single order priced off executions in the related derivative, not a series of market orders. A Special Terms Order (A) is defined by size, conditions or settlement. A Contingent Derivative Order (D) is a separate defined term, not this index-basket structure.
questions.source_quote: "“Program Trade” means a trade resulting from a series of market orders for the purchase or sale of particular securities underlying an index that has been designated by a Market Regulator" — umir.txt ~line 1757

questions.stem: A retail client of Glenbrook Securities repeatedly enters and quickly cancels large visible buy orders in a thinly traded security with no intention that they execute, drawing other buyers in before he sells. Which paragraph of Policy 2.2 Part 2 most directly captures the order entry conduct?

options.A: Entering orders to effect a high closing price
options.B: Making a fictitious trade
options.C: Effecting a trade between accounts under the direction of the same person
options.D: Entering an order or a series of orders for a security that are not intended to be executed

questions.rationale: Policy 2.2 Part 2(f) lists entering an order or a series of orders for a security or a derivative that are not intended to be executed — the classic spoofing/layering pattern here — as a violation of Rule 2.2(2). High-closing (A) is Part 2(e)(ii) and turns on end-of-day price setting, not cancelled orders. A fictitious trade (B) is a Part 1 act requiring an actual trade. Same-person account trades (C) are Part 2(i) wash-trade conduct, also requiring executions, not cancellations.
questions.source_quote: "entering an order or a series of orders for a security or a derivative that are not intended to be executed" — umir.txt ~line 3469

questions.stem: A trader at Gairloch Securities hears a vague market rumour that a large institutional buyer is 'circling' a particular stock and buys for his own account. No client order exists at the firm. Under Policy 4.1 Part 2, his trading:

options.A: Violates Rule 4.1 because rumours are equivalent to knowledge
options.B: Violates Rule 2.1(2)(b) automatically
options.C: Does not constitute frontrunning, because trading based on non-specific pieces of market information, including rumours, requires no specific knowledge of a client order
options.D: Is permitted only if he first reports the rumour to the Market Regulator

questions.rationale: Policy 4.1 Part 2 requires that, to constitute frontrunning, the person must have specific knowledge concerning the client order that on entry could reasonably be expected to affect the market price, and states that trading based on non-specific pieces of market information, including rumours, does not constitute frontrunning. Equating rumours with knowledge (A) contradicts the Policy. Rule 2.1(2)(b) (B) requires knowledge of a client's expression of interest at the Participant, which is absent. There is no rumour-reporting precondition (D) in Rule 4.1.
questions.source_quote: "Trading based on non-specific pieces of market information, including rumours, does not constitute frontrunning." — umir.txt ~line 4680

questions.stem: Under UMIR 5.3(2), which of the following principal or non-client orders is NOT required to give priority to a previously received client order on the same side at the same price?

options.A: An inventory order entered manually by the trader who took the client order
options.B: A principal order entered after the head of trading verbally approves it
options.C: A principal order for a managed account where the client order is for a managed account under the direction of the same person, with executions allocated between the managed accounts on an equitable basis
options.D: Any order under 50 standard trading units

questions.rationale: UMIR 5.3(2)(b)(iii) exempts a principal or non-client order for a managed account where the client order is for a managed account under the direction of the same person and executions are allocated between the various managed accounts on an equitable basis in accordance with the Participant's established practices. A manually entered competing inventory order (A) is the core conduct Rule 5.3(1) prohibits. Head-of-trading approval (B) is not an exemption — only the routes in 5.3(2) are. Order size (D) plays no role in the client priority exemptions.
questions.source_quote: "for a managed account and the client order is for a managed account under the direction of the same person and in respect of which executions are allocated between the various managed accounts on an equitable basis in accordance with the established practices of the Participant" — umir.txt ~line 5064

questions.stem: A client of Hollins Securities instructs that his large order be worked in parts at various times during the trading day. Under UMIR 5.3(3), with respect to the firm entering principal orders in the same security that day, the client:

options.A: Must sign a separate written priority waiver before the order is accepted
options.B: Retains absolute priority over every principal order until his order is fully filled
options.C: Is protected only if the firm's order is larger than his
options.D: Is deemed to have consented to the Participant entering principal and non-client orders, because his order is, on his specific instructions, to be executed in part at various times during the trading day

questions.rationale: UMIR 5.3(3) deems a client to have consented to the Participant entering principal orders and non-client orders for the same security at the same price on the same side where the client order, in accordance with the client's specific instructions, is to be executed in part at various times during the trading day or at various prices. A separate written waiver (A) is not what the rule requires — the deemed consent flows from the over-the-day instruction itself. Absolute priority (B) is displaced by the deeming provision. Relative order size (C) is irrelevant to 5.3(3).
questions.source_quote: "if the client order, in accordance with the specific instructions of the client, is to be executed in part at various times during the trading day or at various prices during the trading day." — umir.txt ~line 5109

questions.stem: A client of Larchmont Securities instructs the firm to withhold her order from the market. Under Policy 5.3 Part 3, for how long must details of the instruction be retained, and where must they be kept initially?

options.A: Five years, in the compliance department's central file
options.B: Seven years from the date of the instruction, and for the first two years in a readily accessible location
options.C: Two years, with the branch manager
options.D: Ten years, in offsite archival storage

questions.rationale: Policy 5.3 Part 3 requires that if a client has instructed a Participant to withhold an order or granted discretion over entry, details of the instruction or grant of discretion must be retained for seven years from the date of the instruction and, for the first two years, the consent must be kept in a readily accessible location. Five years (A) is the Policy 7.1 Part 2 retention period for compliance review results — the closest distractor. Two years (C) is only the readily-accessible window, not the full retention period. Ten years (D) appears nowhere in the provision.
questions.source_quote: "details of the instruction or grant of discretion must be retained for a period of seven years from the date of the instruction or grant of discretion and, for the first two years, the consent must be kept in a readily accessible location." — umir.txt ~line 5181

questions.stem: Under IDPC Rule 5113, the loan value of a long position in a client margin account is generally calculated as:

options.A: [100% + applicable margin rate %] x positive market value of the security
options.B: Applicable margin rate % x market value of the security
options.C: [100% - applicable margin rate %] x positive market value of the security
options.D: 50% of market value in all cases

questions.rationale: IDPC 5113(2) sets the loan value of a long position at [100% - applicable margin rate %] x positive market value of the security. Adding the margin rate (A) is the formula for the loan value of a short position under 5113(3), which uses [100% + applicable margin rate %] times the negative market value — the closest distractor because it inverts the sign. Margin rate times market value (B) is the Dealer Member inventory margin formula under 5112, not client loan value. A flat 50% (D) ignores that margin rates are prescribed by the Corporation and vary by security.
questions.source_quote: "[100% - applicable margin rate %] x positive market value of the security" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 12067

questions.stem: A client of Quenby Securities owes the firm a margin debt. Under IDPC Rule 5114, which securities held by the firm for that client are collateral for payment of the debt?

options.A: Only securities the client has expressly pledged in writing
options.B: All securities the Dealer Member holds for the client, up to an amount that reasonably covers the margin debt
options.C: Only securities purchased on margin in that account
options.D: All securities in the account without any limit tied to the debt

questions.rationale: IDPC 5114(1) provides that if a client is in debt to a Dealer Member, all securities the Dealer Member holds for the client, up to an amount that reasonably covers the margin debt, are collateral for payment of the debt. An express written pledge (A) is not the trigger — the rule operates on the debt itself, subject to Form 1 Schedule 4 and any agreement. Limiting collateral to margin-purchased securities (C) is narrower than the rule. Unlimited collateralization (D) ignores the 'reasonably covers' cap.
questions.source_quote: "If a client is in debt to a Dealer Member, all securities the Dealer Member holds for the client, up to an amount that reasonably covers the margin debt, are collateral for payment of the debt." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 12084

questions.stem: A client of Oakhurst Securities acquires a position in an unusual security for which no margin rate or requirement is specified within the Corporation requirements. Under IDPC Rule 5120, the Dealer Member must:

options.A: Apply a default margin rate of 100%
options.B: Apply the margin rate of the most similar listed security
options.C: Obtain a margin rule interpretation from Corporation staff specifying the margin rate or requirement to be used
options.D: Exclude the position from margin calculations until a rate is published

questions.rationale: IDPC 5120(1) requires that where a security position is held in inventory or a client account for which a margin rate or requirement is not specified within the Corporation requirements, the Dealer Member must obtain a margin rule interpretation from Corporation staff specifying the rate or requirement to be used. A default 100% rate (A) is a plausible conservative practice but is not what the rule prescribes. Analogizing to a similar security (B) substitutes the dealer's judgment for the required interpretation. Excluding the position (D) would understate margin entirely.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must obtain a margin rule interpretation from Corporation staff specifying the margin rate or requirement to be used." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 12108

questions.stem: Which disclosure does IDPC Rule 3816(2) require on a written trade confirmation for a trade in securities?

options.A: The client's risk profile and investment objectives
options.B: The Registered Representative's compensation grid
options.C: The names of all other clients who traded the security that day
options.D: Whether or not the person or company that executed the trade acted as principal or agent

questions.rationale: IDPC 3816(2)(i)(c) requires the confirmation for trades in securities to state whether or not the person or company that executed the trade acted as principal or agent, alongside quantity and description, consideration, marketplace, regulatory fees, salesperson name and settlement date. Risk profile and objectives (A) are KYC records, not confirmation content. The RR's grid (B) is internal compensation information. Other clients' trading (C) would breach confidentiality and is not a confirmation element.
questions.source_quote: "whether or not the person or company that executed the trade acted as principal or agent" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7526

questions.stem: The desk at Brindley Securities receives instructions to cross a block between two clients at $9.00 when the best bid is $10.00 — a price about 10% below the best bid and more than 10 trading increments away. Under UMIR 2.1(3), before entering the order intended to execute as this intentional cross, the desk must:

options.A: Obtain the prior approval of a Market Regulator
options.B: Simply mark the order as a Special Terms Order
options.C: Wait until the last 15 minutes of trading
options.D: Obtain the written consent of the issuer

questions.rationale: UMIR 2.1(3) prohibits entering an order intended to execute as a pre-arranged trade or intentional cross without the prior approval of a Market Regulator if the price would be less than the lesser of 95% of the best bid price and the best bid price less 10 trading increments — a $9.00 cross against a $10.00 bid breaches both limbs. A Special Terms marker (B) does not substitute for regulator approval. Timing the entry late in the day (C) has no exempting effect. Issuer consent (D) plays no role in UMIR pre-arranged trade approval.
questions.source_quote: "A Participant or Access Person shall not enter an order on a marketplace that is intended to execute as a pre-arranged trade or an intentional cross without the prior approval of a Market Regulator if the pre-arranged trade or intentional cross would be undertaken at a price that will be:" — umir.txt ~line 2888

questions.stem: A trader at Castell Securities tries to enter a buy order at $12.503 for a security trading around $12.50. Under UMIR 6.1(1), the order entry desk should reject it because:

options.A: Orders cannot be priced within one cent of the last sale price
options.B: No order may be entered on a marketplace at a price that includes a fraction of a cent, other than a half-cent increment for an order priced below $0.50
options.C: Three-decimal pricing is allowed only for Dark Orders
options.D: Buy orders must always be entered at whole-dollar prices

questions.rationale: UMIR 6.1(1) provides that no order shall be entered to trade on a marketplace at a price that includes a fraction or a part of a cent other than an increment of one-half of one cent in respect of an order with a price of less than $0.50 — at $12.503 the sub-penny increment is impermissible. Proximity to the last sale (A) is not a pricing restriction. Dark Orders (C) are about display, not sub-penny entry; only an intentional cross under 6.1(3) may execute at a fractional increment when both sides get a better price. Whole-dollar pricing (D) is invented.
questions.source_quote: "No order to purchase or sell a security shall be entered to trade on a marketplace at a price that includes a fraction or a part of a cent other than an increment of one-half of one cent in respect of an order with a price of less than $0.50." — umir.txt ~line 5390

questions.stem: An order entry clerk at Thornbury Securities is preparing a bundled order for entry and asks whether the order must also carry the client identifier required by UMIR 6.2(1)(a)(iv). Under UMIR 6.2(1)(c):

options.A: The client identifier is always required in addition to the bundled order designation
options.B: Bundled orders are prohibited from entry on a marketplace
options.C: The client identifier may be replaced by the head of trading's identifier
options.D: Where the bundled order or multiple client order designation is required, the Participant does not need to include a client identifier on the order

questions.rationale: UMIR 6.2(1)(c) provides that where a designation is required under 6.2(1)(b)(xx) (bundled order) or (xxi) (multiple client order), the Participant does not need to include a client identifier on the order under 6.2(1)(a)(iv) — the combined-order designations substitute because a single client identifier cannot describe several interests. Requiring both (A) contradicts the express relief. Bundled orders are a recognized designation, not prohibited (B). Substituting a supervisor's identifier (C) has no basis in Rule 6.2.
questions.source_quote: "Where a designation is required under 6.2(1)(b)(xx) or (xxi), the Participant does not need to include a client identifier on the order under 6.2(1)(a)(iv)." — umir.txt ~line 5584

questions.stem: A retail client of Umberley Securities places a single order for 40 standard trading units of a liquid security worth $130,000. The desk asks whether the order exposure rule compels immediate entry for display. Under UMIR 6.3(1)(f), the order:

options.A: Must be displayed immediately because it is 50 standard trading units or less
options.B: Is excepted from the immediate-display requirement because the order has a value of more than $100,000
options.C: Must be split into smaller pieces and each displayed immediately
options.D: May be withheld only with the Market Regulator's consent

questions.rationale: UMIR 6.3(1) requires immediate entry for display of client orders of 50 standard trading units or less unless an exception applies, and clause (f) excepts an order with a value of more than $100,000 — this $130,000 order qualifies even though it is under 50 units. Relying on unit count alone (A) ignores the value exception. Splitting and displaying pieces (C) is not required; the exception removes the display obligation. Market Regulator consent (D) is not how the 6.3(1) exceptions operate.
questions.source_quote: "the order has a value of more than $100,000" — umir.txt ~line 5915

questions.stem: Vantage Securities discovers that 300 shares of a client's executed buy order were posted to the wrong client's account. To fix the error, the firm proposes to move the shares between the accounts by journal entry rather than entering offsetting orders on a marketplace. Under UMIR 6.4(2)(c), this is:

options.A: Prohibited because all transfers must occur on a marketplace
options.B: Permitted only with the consent of both clients' Registered Representatives
options.C: Permitted — Rule 6.4(1) does not apply to a trade to adjust by a journal entry an error in connection with a client order
options.D: Permitted only if the shares are first sold to the firm's inventory account

questions.rationale: UMIR 6.4(2)(c) exempts from the trades-on-a-marketplace requirement a trade to adjust by a journal entry an error in connection with a client order — the precise mechanism proposed. An absolute marketplace requirement (A) ignores the enumerated exemptions in 6.4(2). RR consent (B) is not a UMIR condition for the error-adjustment exemption. Routing through inventory (D) is unnecessary and, if used to re-print the trade, could raise double-printing concerns under Rule 2.2 guidance.
questions.source_quote: "to adjust by a journal entry an error in connection with a client order" — umir.txt ~line 6060

questions.stem: After marketplace hours, Marwood Securities agrees as principal to trade a listed security with a Canadian institutional account, conditional on executing the trade on a marketplace at the next opening. At the opening the cross cannot be completed at the agreed price. Under Policy 6.4 Part 1, Wexford must:

options.A: Book the trade through its error account to preserve the transaction
options.B: Cancel the agreement to trade — and the use of an error account to preserve the transaction is prohibited
options.C: Execute the trade over-the-counter at the agreed price
options.D: Hold the agreement open until conditions allow execution later in the week

questions.rationale: Policy 6.4 Part 1 provides that an after-hours agreement to trade with a Canadian account must be conditional on execution on a marketplace or foreign organized regulated market at or immediately following the opening; if the Participant determines that the condition cannot be met, the agreement to trade shall be cancelled, and use of an error account to preserve the transaction is prohibited — making (A) the directly prohibited act. OTC execution (C) would breach Rule 6.4(1) absent an exemption. Holding the agreement open for days (D) defeats the at-or-immediately-following-the-opening condition.
questions.source_quote: "If the Participant determines that the condition of recording the agreement to trade on a marketplace or foreign organized regulated market cannot be met, the agreement to trade shall be cancelled. Use of an error account to preserve the transaction is prohibited." — umir.txt ~line 6140

questions.stem: A Registered Representative at Aldercroft Securities wants to personally take the other side of his retail client's sell order. Under Policy 8.1 Part 2, before the trade the RR must ensure that the client:

options.A: Has signed a general consent covering all future trades with the Registered Representative
options.B: Holds the security in a margin account
options.C: Is informed and consents to the trade in advance, with the consent specific to that trade — it cannot be a general consent to any future trades with the Registered Representative
options.D: Receives a commission rebate of at least 50%

questions.rationale: Policy 8.1 Part 2 states that if the Registered Representative wishes to take the other side of the trade with their client, the client must be informed and consent to the trade in advance, and such consent must be specific to that trade and cannot be in a general consent to any future trades with the Registered Representative — making the blanket consent in (A) exactly what the Policy forbids. Account type (B) is irrelevant to the consent requirement. A commission rebate (D) is not a Policy 8.1 condition; in fact a higher-than-agency commission makes the principal trade harder to justify.
questions.source_quote: "Such consent must be specific to that trade and cannot be in a general consent to any future trades with the Registered Representative." — umir.txt ~line 9110

questions.stem: Denholm Securities hires a new assistant who has not completed any trading proficiency requirement. The desk Supervisor is asked whether the assistant can begin entering marketplace orders right away. Under UMIR 7.2(1), no order may be entered by the Participant unless the person entering or responsible for the order has:

options.A: Worked on a trading desk for at least two years
options.B: Passed the firm's internal ethics quiz
options.C: Obtained personal approval from the firm's board of directors
options.D: Completed the Trader Training Course of the Canadian Securities Institute, an acceptable equivalent demonstration of proficiency in UMIR, or approval of an Exchange or QTRS for order entry

questions.rationale: UMIR 7.2(1) prohibits the entry of an order unless the Participant or the director, officer, partner or employee entering or responsible for the order has completed the Trader Training Course of the Canadian Securities Institute or such other acceptable demonstration of proficiency in UMIR and Policies, or has received approval of an Exchange or QTRS for the entry of orders. Tenure (A), an internal quiz (B) and board sign-off (C) are not recognized proficiency routes — the rule fixes the course/acceptable-equivalent/exchange-approval standard.
questions.source_quote: "completed the Trader Training Course of the Canadian Securities Institute" — umir.txt ~line 8004

questions.stem: Under UMIR 7.3(2), a Participant is responsible for bids and offers that originate from a terminal or computer system allowing access to trading on a marketplace that is operated by or under the control of the Participant:

options.A: Only if a registered employee personally keyed the order
options.B: Whether or not the Participant has authorized the entry of the order
options.C: Only during regular trading hours
options.D: Only after the trade has settled

questions.rationale: UMIR 7.3(2) makes a Participant responsible for all bids and offers entered into, or arising by operation of the trading system of a marketplace, that originate from any terminal or computer system allowing access to trading that is operated by or under the control of the Participant, whether or not the Participant has authorized the entry of the order. Limiting responsibility to keyed orders (A) ignores system-originated orders. Trading-hours (C) and post-settlement (D) limits are invented — responsibility attaches at entry, irrespective of authorization.
questions.source_quote: "A Participant shall be responsible for all bids and offers that are entered into, or arise by operation of the trading system of a marketplace and that originate from any terminal or computer system allowing access to trading on the marketplace" — umir.txt ~line 8045

questions.stem: Elphin Securities has retained an independent third party under UMIR 7.1(7) to provide its electronic trading risk management controls. Under UMIR 7.1(10)(b), how often must Eastvale review and confirm the adequacy and consistent application of the third party's controls and its compliance with the written agreement?

options.A: Monthly
options.B: Quarterly
options.C: At least annually, by the anniversary date of the written agreement with the third party
options.D: Only when the third party reports a malfunction

questions.rationale: UMIR 7.1(10)(b) requires the Participant that has retained a third party (or authorized an investment dealer) to review and confirm, at least annually by the anniversary date of the written agreement, that the controls are adequate, have been maintained and consistently applied, that deficiencies were documented and promptly remedied, and that the third party complies with the written agreement. Monthly (A) and quarterly (B) frequencies belong to other Policy 7.1 testing cycles (trade-through testing, sampling reviews), not the third-party confirmation. Waiting for a malfunction report (D) ignores the affirmative annual obligation.
questions.source_quote: "at least annually by the anniversary date of the written agreement with the investment dealer or third party that" — umir.txt ~line 6621

questions.stem: Tillsdale Capital terminates its written agreement with the third party that had been providing its risk management and supervisory controls under UMIR 7.1. Under UMIR 10.17, Ferncliff must:

options.A: Forthwith report the termination of the written agreement to the Market Regulator
options.B: Report the termination in its next annual filing
options.C: Do nothing, as terminations are a private commercial matter
options.D: Obtain the Market Regulator's approval before terminating

questions.rationale: UMIR 10.17(1)(a) requires a Participant that has authorized an investment dealer or used a third party under Rule 7.1 to forthwith report to the Market Regulator the fact that the written agreement has been terminated; 10.17(1)(b) extends the same forthwith reporting to knowledge that the provider failed to promptly remedy a deficiency. Annual-filing timing (B) misses the forthwith standard. Silence (C) ignores the gatekeeper reporting duty. Pre-approval of the termination (D) is not required — the obligation is to report, not to seek consent.
questions.source_quote: "the written agreement with the investment dealer or third party has been terminated" — umir.txt ~line 10708

questions.stem: Hollybrae Securities granted direct electronic access to a qualifying client eighteen months ago and has not revisited the file since. Under Policy 7.1 Part 9, what ongoing confirmation does the Market Regulator require of the firm?

options.A: Re-screening only when the client requests increased limits
options.B: Confirmation at least annually that the client continues to meet the standards established by the Participant, including that any modification to a previously approved automated order system maintains appropriate safeguards
options.C: A new written agreement every six months
options.D: Daily re-validation of the client's financial resources

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 9 requires the Participant to confirm with the client granted direct electronic access (or an investment dealer or foreign dealer equivalent in a routing arrangement), at least annually, that the client continues to meet the standards established by the Participant, including ensuring any modification to a previously approved automated order system continues to maintain appropriate safeguards. Event-driven re-screening only (A) misses the affirmative annual cycle. A six-month agreement refresh (C) and daily financial re-validation (D) are not what the Policy prescribes.
questions.source_quote: "The Participant is further required to confirm with the client granted direct electronic access or an investment dealer or foreign dealer equivalent in a routing arrangement, at least annually, that the client, investment dealer or foreign dealer equivalent continues to meet the standards established by the Participant" — umir.txt ~line 7041

questions.stem: Under Policy 7.1 Part 5, how frequently, at a minimum, must a Participant review a sample of its trading for manipulative and deceptive activities?

options.A: At least daily
options.B: At least monthly
options.C: At least quarterly
options.D: At least annually

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 5 requires each Participant to review a sample of its trading for manipulative and deceptive activities at least on a quarterly basis. Daily review (A) is the frequency Policy 7.1 Part 12 prescribes for Grey/Watch and Restricted list trading — the closest distractor. Monthly (B) is the Part 6 minimum for testing trade-through policies and procedures. Annual (D) is the Part 2 supervision-system review cycle, not the manipulative-activity sampling cycle.
questions.source_quote: "Each Participant must review a sample of its trading for manipulative and deceptive activities at least on a quarterly basis." — umir.txt ~line 6912
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 1 wave 6 (2026-06-05): solver answered B (monthly) vs key C (quarterly), medium confidence — swapped the Policy 7.1 Part 5 (quarterly manipulative-sample) and Part 6 (monthly trade-through-test) frequencies. Key is rule-supported verbatim, but solver-wrong = rewrite-or-kill, no exceptions. Angle queued for E7-003 with structurally different framing (not a bare frequency pick).

questions.stem: Dunmore Securities marks certain orders as directed action orders. Under Policy 7.1 Part 6, how often, at a minimum, must the firm test that its trade-through prevention policies and procedures are adequate?

options.A: Not less than monthly
options.B: Not less than quarterly
options.C: Not less than annually
options.D: Only after a trade-through alert is received

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 6 requires the policies and procedures regarding directed action orders and trade-throughs to address the steps the Participant or Access Person will undertake on a regular basis, which shall not be less than monthly, to test that the policies and procedures are adequate. Quarterly (B) is the cycle for manipulative-activity sampling, audit trail, order handling and trade confirmation reviews under Parts 5, 10, 11 and 13. Annual (C) is the supervision-system review. Alert-driven testing only (D) ignores the affirmative monthly minimum.
questions.source_quote: "These policies and procedures must address the steps which the Participant or Access Person will undertake on a regular basis, which shall not be less than monthly, to test that the policies and procedures are adequate." — umir.txt ~line 6930
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 1 wave 6 (2026-06-05): solver answered B (quarterly) vs key A (monthly), LOW confidence + ambiguity flag (quarterly vs annually both defensible from memory). Mirror confusion with SUP-E7-0078 (mutual closest-distractor pair drafted together). Key is rule-supported verbatim (Policy 7.1 Part 6 'not less than monthly'), but solver-wrong + low confidence = kill. Angle queued for E7-003.

questions.stem: Under Policy 7.1 Part 10, how must the sample sets for a Participant's audit trail compliance reviews be constructed, and how often must those reviews occur?

options.A: Drawn only from the firm's largest trading desk, monthly
options.B: Randomly selected to proportionately cover orders and trades related to all of the Participant's lines of business, with reviews at least quarterly
options.C: Limited to orders flagged by surveillance alerts, annually
options.D: Selected by the head of trading at their discretion, semi-annually

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 10 requires that sample sets be randomly selected to proportionately cover orders and trades related to all lines of business of a Participant, with reviews for compliance with Audit Trail Requirements carried out at least quarterly (and Record Retention reviews at least annually). Concentrating on one desk (A) defeats proportional coverage. Alert-only sampling (C) is not random and the annual frequency belongs to record-retention reviews. Discretionary selection by the head of trading (D) contradicts both the randomness requirement and the separation of compliance from trading supervision.
questions.source_quote: "Sample sets must be randomly selected to proportionately cover orders and trades related to all lines of business of a Participant." — umir.txt ~line 7112

questions.stem: Under Policy 7.1 Part 11, a Participant's compliance reviews of order handling must, at a minimum, include which check — and on what cycle?

options.A: Verifying that client orders of 50 standard trading units or less are not withheld from the market without a valid exemption from the order exposure rule, with review at least quarterly
options.B: Verifying that all orders execute within one minute of receipt, daily
options.C: Verifying commission rates against the published schedule, monthly
options.D: Verifying short positions against custody records, annually

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 11 requires the supervision system to ensure trading does not violate order exposure (Rule 6.3) or client-principal requirements, with reviews that at a minimum include verifying that client orders of 50 standard trading units or less are not withheld from the market without a valid exemption from the order exposure rule and reviewing client-principal trades of 50 units/$100,000 or less — and the order entry and trading must be reviewed at least quarterly. A one-minute execution standard (B), commission audits (C) and custody reconciliation (D) are not Part 11 elements.
questions.source_quote: "verifying that client orders of 50 standard trading units or less are not withheld from the market without a valid exemption from order exposure rule" — umir.txt ~line 7123

questions.stem: Under Policy 7.1 Part 13, how often must a Participant review a sample of trade confirmations to verify that appropriate trade disclosures are made to clients?

options.A: At least daily
options.B: At least monthly
options.C: At least annually
options.D: At least quarterly

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 13 requires each Participant to maintain a supervision system verifying that appropriate trade disclosures — quantity and description, principal-or-agent capacity, consideration, related issuers, trade date and marketplace, salesperson and settlement date — are made on client confirmations, and to review a sample of trade confirmations at least on a quarterly basis. Daily (A) is the Grey/Restricted list review frequency under Part 12. Monthly (B) is the trade-through testing minimum under Part 6. Annual (C) is the supervision-system review cycle under Part 2.
questions.source_quote: "Each Participant must review a sample of trade confirmations at least on a quarterly basis." — umir.txt ~line 7166

questions.stem: Inverlea Securities executes purchases for an issuer's normal course issuer bid. Under Policy 7.1 Part 14, the firm's supervision system must review NCIB-related trading to ensure, among other things, that:

options.A: All NCIB purchases occur in the final hour of trading
options.B: NCIB purchases are entered only as Dark Orders
options.C: NCIB purchases are not made on upticks, and maximum daily and annual stock purchase limits are observed
options.D: NCIB purchases occur only while a sale from control for the same security is in effect

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 14 requires the supervision system to review NCIB-related trading to ensure maximum daily and annual stock purchase limits are observed, purchases do not occur while a sale from control for the same security is in effect, NCIB purchases are not made on upticks, and NCIB trade reporting to the Exchange is made where the firm reports for the issuer. A final-hour requirement (A) and a Dark Order mandate (B) are invented. Option (D) reverses the Policy — purchases must NOT occur while a sale from control is in effect.
questions.source_quote: "NCIB purchases are not made on upticks" — umir.txt ~line 7176

questions.stem: Under UMIR 10.12, a Participant must retain the record of each order required by Rule 10.11 and sufficient information to identify the beneficial owner of each account:

options.A: For not less than seven years from the creation of the record, with the first two years in a readily accessible location
options.B: For five years from the trade date, archived offsite immediately
options.C: For two years, after which the records may be destroyed
options.D: Until the next regulatory examination is completed

questions.rationale: UMIR 10.12(1) requires retention of the record of each order under Rule 10.11 and sufficient information to identify the beneficial owner of each account for a period of not less than seven years from the creation of the record of the order, and for the first two years such record and information shall be kept in a readily accessible location. Five years (B) is the Policy 7.1 Part 2 retention period for compliance review results — the closest distractor. Two years (C) is only the readily-accessible window. Examination-based retention (D) has no basis in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "for a period of not less than seven years from the creation of the record of the order, and for the first two years, such record and information shall be kept in a readily accessible location." — umir.txt ~line 10382

questions.stem: A compliance reviewer at Seabright Securities checks recorded prices on agency trades. Under UMIR 7.5(1), a Participant acting as agent must not execute a transaction through a marketplace in which the price recorded on the marketplace is:

options.A: Different from the volume-weighted average price for the day
options.B: In the case of a purchase by a client, higher than the net cost to the client, or, in a sale by a client, lower than the net proceeds to the client
options.C: Below the previous day's closing price
options.D: Equal to the client's limit price

questions.rationale: UMIR 7.5(1) prohibits a Participant acting as agent from executing a transaction in which the price recorded on the marketplace is, for a client purchase, higher than the net cost to the client, or, for a client sale, lower than the net proceeds to the client — the recorded price cannot disadvantage the client relative to their actual economics. VWAP comparisons (A) and prior-close benchmarks (C) are not the Rule 7.5 test. Executing at the client's limit price (D) is normal and permitted, not prohibited.
questions.source_quote: "in the case of a purchase by a client, higher than the net cost to the client" — umir.txt ~line 8128

questions.stem: Under Policy 7.1 Part 5, which monitoring step must a Participant's manipulative-and-deceptive supervision procedures specifically address with respect to clients holding several accounts?

options.A: Consolidating all of a client's accounts into one for trading purposes
options.B: The steps to be taken to monitor the trading activity of any person who has multiple accounts with the Participant, including other accounts in which the person has an interest or over which the person has direction or control
options.C: Closing any account that has been dormant for a year
options.D: Prohibiting clients from holding more than two accounts

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 5 requires the policies and procedures to address the steps to be taken to monitor the trading activity of any person who has multiple accounts with the Participant including other accounts in which the person has an interest or over which the person has direction or control — multi-account monitoring is how wash trading and same-person crosses are caught. Account consolidation (A), dormancy closures (C) and an account-count cap (D) are operational choices the Policy nowhere requires; the obligation is monitoring, not restructuring.
questions.source_quote: "the steps to be taken to monitor the trading activity of any person who has multiple accounts with the Participant including other accounts in which the person has an interest or over which the person has direction or control" — umir.txt ~line 6884

questions.stem: Per Policy 7.1 Part 1, when an order is entered on a marketplace by direct electronic access, under a routing arrangement or through an order execution service, the Participant:

options.A: Retains responsibility for that order, and its supervision policies should address the additional risk exposure of orders not directly handled by its staff
options.B: Transfers responsibility for the order to the client
options.C: Is responsible only for orders the Market Regulator later questions
options.D: Is responsible only if the order exceeds pre-set credit limits

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 1 states that when an order is entered by direct electronic access, under a routing arrangement or through an order execution service, the Participant retains responsibility for that order, and the supervision policies and procedures should adequately address the additional risk exposure for orders not directly handled by staff of the Participant. Shifting responsibility to the client (B) contradicts the retained-responsibility principle. Responsibility is not contingent on regulator inquiries (C) or limit breaches (D) — it attaches to every such order.
questions.source_quote: "When an order is entered on a marketplace by direct electronic access, under a routing arrangement or through an order execution service, the Participant retains responsibility for that order" — umir.txt ~line 6683

questions.stem: Per Policy 7.1 Part 1, which of the following is an example of a "post order entry" compliance test focused on orders entered by clients under direct electronic access?

options.A: Confirming the client's annual income remains accurate
options.B: Re-papering the direct electronic access agreement
options.C: Testing whether an order is part of a wash trade, in circumstances where the client has more than one account with the Participant
options.D: Verifying the client paid its commissions on time

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 1 lists post-order-entry compliance testing examples for orders entered under direct electronic access, routing arrangements or order execution services: whether the order has created an artificial price contrary to Rule 2.2; is part of a wash trade where the client has more than one account with the Participant; is an unmarked short sale; and has complied with other order marking requirements including the insider and significant shareholder markers. Income verification (A) is KYC maintenance, agreement re-papering (B) is the Part 9 annual confirmation process, and commission collection (D) is a credit matter — none is post-order-entry testing.
questions.source_quote: "is part of a “wash trade” (in circumstances where the client has more than one account with the Participant)" — umir.txt ~line 6701

questions.stem: A compliance officer at Roseharbour Securities receives an internal report under UMIR 10.16(1). Under UMIR 10.16(3), what must the supervisor or compliance department do upon receiving the report?

options.A: Immediately file a gatekeeper report with the Market Regulator before any internal review
options.B: Diligently conduct a review in accordance with the policies and procedures the Participant adopted under Rule 7.1
options.C: Suspend the employee who made the report pending investigation
options.D: Refer the matter directly to the police

questions.rationale: UMIR 10.16(3) requires that if a supervisor or compliance department receives a report under subsection (1) or (2), they shall diligently conduct a review in accordance with the policies and procedures of the Participant adopted in accordance with Rule 7.1 (or the ordinary practices of the Access Person). Immediate external filing (A) reverses the sequence — the Market Regulator report under 10.16(4)(d) follows only after the review concludes there may be a violation, an investigation occurs and a violation is found. Suspending the reporting employee (C) punishes the gatekeeper. A police referral (D) is not the prescribed first step.
questions.source_quote: "If a supervisor or compliance department of a Participant or Access Person receives a report pursuant to subsection (1) or (2), the supervisor or compliance department shall diligently conduct a review in accordance with the policies and procedures of the Participant adopted in accordance with Rule 7.1" — umir.txt ~line 10584

questions.stem: An analyst employed by an Access Person notices that the Access Person has been entering short sale orders with no apparent ability to obtain the securities, which she believes may violate the Rule 3.3(1) requirement of a reasonable expectation to settle. Under UMIR 10.16(2), she must:

options.A: Do nothing — the gatekeeper rule applies only to employees of Participants
options.B: Report to the Market Regulator directly within 30 days
options.C: Wait until the activity recurs in a second account
options.D: Forthwith report to her supervisor or the compliance department of the Access Person

questions.rationale: UMIR 10.16(2) requires an officer, director, partner or employee of an Access Person to forthwith report to their supervisor or the compliance department of the Access Person upon becoming aware of activity by the Access Person or a related entity that they believe may be a violation of enumerated provisions, including subsection (1) of Rule 3.3 respecting the reasonable expectation to settle a short sale. Limiting the duty to Participant employees (A) ignores subsection (2). Direct Market Regulator reporting (B) is the firm's later 10.16(4)(d) step. Waiting for recurrence (C) breaches the forthwith standard.
questions.source_quote: "An officer, director, partner or employee of an Access Person shall forthwith report to their supervisor or the compliance department of the Access Person upon becoming aware of activity by the Access Person or a related entity" — umir.txt ~line 10571

questions.stem: Per Policy 1.2 Part 4, the duty to "use due diligence to learn and remain informed of the essential facts relative to every customer and to every order or account accepted" is described as:

options.A: Relevant only to the suitability of investments for a client
options.B: A central component of the Gatekeeper Obligation embodied within the trading supervision obligation under Rules 7.1 and 10.16
options.C: An optional best practice for institutional desks
options.D: A duty owed exclusively to the issuer of the security

questions.rationale: Policy 1.2 Part 4 explains that while knowledge of essential facts of every customer and order is necessary to determine suitability, the requirement is not limited to that single application — the exercise of due diligence to learn essential facts relative to every customer and to every order is a central component of the Gatekeeper Obligation embodied within the trading supervision obligation under Rule 7.1 and 10.16. Confining the duty to suitability (A) is exactly the narrowing the Policy rejects. It is mandatory, not optional (C), and is owed in the supervision context, not to issuers (D).
questions.source_quote: "use due diligence to learn and remain informed of the essential facts relative to every customer and to every order or account accepted." — umir.txt ~line 2724

questions.scenario: Tomas Reyes supervises the trading desk at Rivelin Securities, a CIRO Participant. A hedge fund client, Pelican Narrows Gold's largest outside holder, calls the desk wanting to sell 80,000 shares of Pelican Narrows Gold Corp. that it does not currently hold in its account, expecting to receive borrowed shares from its prime broker later in the week. The desk has no securities lending arrangement in place for the order, and Pelican Narrows Gold has recently appeared on lists of securities with elevated settlement failures. Two weeks earlier, a different sale by the same client in another security failed to settle, and the shares were still not delivered ten trading days after the contemplated settlement date. A junior desk assistant also mentions to Reyes that she saw the order ticket prepared without any short sale designation.
questions.stem: The hedge fund's sale order would, on execution, be a short sale. The junior assistant saw the ticket prepared with no short sale designation. Under UMIR 3.2(1)(a), the desk may not enter the order on a marketplace:

options.A: Unless the order is marked as a short sale in accordance with subclause 6.2(1)(b)(viii)
options.B: Unless the client first deposits 150% margin
options.C: Unless the issuer consents to the short sale
options.D: Until the firm publishes the short position in a consolidated market display

questions.rationale: UMIR 3.2(1)(a) prohibits entering an order to sell that on execution would be a short sale unless the order is marked as a short sale in accordance with subclause 6.2(1)(b)(viii) (subject only to the short-marking-exempt designation in 3.2(2)). Margin levels (B) are governed by the IDPC margin rules, not Rule 3.2. Issuer consent (C) has no role in short sale marking. Public display of short positions (D) confuses the order marker with the separate Rule 10.10 short position reporting regime.
questions.source_quote: "unless the order is marked as a short sale in accordance with subclause 6.2(1)(b)(viii)" — umir.txt ~line 4471

questions.scenario: Tomas Reyes supervises the trading desk at Rivelin Securities, a CIRO Participant. A hedge fund client, Pelican Narrows Gold's largest outside holder, calls the desk wanting to sell 80,000 shares of Pelican Narrows Gold Corp. that it does not currently hold in its account, expecting to receive borrowed shares from its prime broker later in the week. The desk has no securities lending arrangement in place for the order, and Pelican Narrows Gold has recently appeared on lists of securities with elevated settlement failures. Two weeks earlier, a different sale by the same client in another security failed to settle, and the shares were still not delivered ten trading days after the contemplated settlement date. A junior desk assistant also mentions to Reyes that she saw the order ticket prepared without any short sale designation.
questions.stem: The client does not hold the shares and the desk has no borrowing arrangement in place for the order, in a security with elevated settlement failures. Under UMIR 3.3(1), before entering the order the desk must have:

options.A: The Market Regulator's written pre-clearance for the short sale
options.B: A guarantee from the client's prime broker addressed to the marketplace
options.C: A reasonable expectation to settle any resulting trade on the date contemplated on the execution of the trade
options.D: Confirmation that the security is on the firm's Grey list

questions.rationale: UMIR 3.3(1) requires that before entering an order that on execution would result in a short sale, the Participant or Access Person must have a reasonable expectation to settle any resulting trade on the date contemplated on the execution of the trade — on these facts that expectation is doubtful and must be established before entry. Market Regulator pre-clearance (A) is not required for ordinary short sales. A prime-broker guarantee to the marketplace (B) is not the rule's mechanism, though borrowing arrangements can support the expectation. Grey list status (D) concerns inside-information monitoring, not settlement.
questions.source_quote: "Before entering an order on a marketplace to sell a security that on execution would result in a short sale, a Participant or Access Person must have a reasonable expectation to settle any resulting trade on the date contemplated on the execution of the trade." — umir.txt ~line 4542

questions.scenario: Tomas Reyes supervises the trading desk at Rivelin Securities, a CIRO Participant. A hedge fund client, Pelican Narrows Gold's largest outside holder, calls the desk wanting to sell 80,000 shares of Pelican Narrows Gold Corp. that it does not currently hold in its account, expecting to receive borrowed shares from its prime broker later in the week. The desk has no securities lending arrangement in place for the order, and Pelican Narrows Gold has recently appeared on lists of securities with elevated settlement failures. Two weeks earlier, a different sale by the same client in another security failed to settle, and the shares were still not delivered ten trading days after the contemplated settlement date. A junior desk assistant also mentions to Reyes that she saw the order ticket prepared without any short sale designation.
questions.stem: The client's earlier sale in another security failed to settle and the shares were still undelivered ten trading days after the contemplated settlement date. Under UMIR 7.10(1), at that point Quillan, as the Participant that entered the order, was required to:

options.A: Cancel the original trade and re-execute it
options.B: Quietly close the client's account
options.C: Buy in the position using the firm's error account
options.D: Give notice to the Market Regulator at such time and in such form and manner and containing such information as the Market Regulator requires

questions.rationale: UMIR 7.10(1) provides that if within ten trading days following the date for settlement contemplated on execution the account has not made available the securities or monies (or borrowing arrangements, for a short sale), the Participant or Access Person that entered the order shall give notice to the Market Regulator in the required time, form and manner — the extended failed trade report. Post-execution cancellation (A) is restricted by Rule 7.11. Closing the account (B) does not discharge the reporting duty. An error-account buy-in (C) is not the prescribed regulatory response.
questions.source_quote: "the Participant or Access Person that entered the order on a marketplace shall give notice to the Market Regulator at such time and in such form and manner and containing such information as may be required by the Market Regulator." — umir.txt ~line 8670

questions.scenario: Tomas Reyes supervises the trading desk at Rivelin Securities, a CIRO Participant. A hedge fund client, Pelican Narrows Gold's largest outside holder, calls the desk wanting to sell 80,000 shares of Pelican Narrows Gold Corp. that it does not currently hold in its account, expecting to receive borrowed shares from its prime broker later in the week. The desk has no securities lending arrangement in place for the order, and Pelican Narrows Gold has recently appeared on lists of securities with elevated settlement failures. Two weeks earlier, a different sale by the same client in another security failed to settle, and the shares were still not delivered ten trading days after the contemplated settlement date. A junior desk assistant also mentions to Reyes that she saw the order ticket prepared without any short sale designation.
questions.stem: The junior desk assistant believes the order may be entered without the required reasonable expectation to settle. Under UMIR 10.16(1), her belief engages the employee gatekeeper reporting obligation because the enumerated provisions include:

options.A: Subsection (1) of Rule 3.3 respecting a reasonable expectation to settle prior to the entry of an order for a short sale
options.B: Rule 10.14 respecting synchronization of clocks
options.C: Rule 7.5 respecting recorded prices
options.D: Rule 10.12 respecting retention of records

questions.rationale: UMIR 10.16(1)(c.1) enumerates subsection (1) of Rule 3.3 respecting a reasonable expectation to settle prior to the entry of an order for a short sale among the provisions for which an officer, director, partner or employee must forthwith report potentially violative account activity to their supervisor or the compliance department. Clock synchronization (B), recorded prices (C) and record retention (D) are compliance and administrative provisions that do not appear in the 10.16(1) enumeration.
questions.source_quote: "Subsection (1) of Rule 3.3 respecting a reasonable expectation to settle prior to the entry of an order for a short sale" — umir.txt ~line 10560

questions.scenario: Maren Holt is the designated trading Supervisor at Tobermory Securities, a small CIRO Dealer Member that executes retail client orders in listed securities across several Canadian marketplaces. During an internal review, Holt finds: (1) the firm's written policies and procedures say nothing specific about how best execution is achieved for client orders; (2) a cost-saving proposal on her desk recommends sending all client orders in listed securities in bulk to a U.S. intermediary for execution without looking at Canadian liquidity sources; (3) the best execution policies and procedures were last reviewed three years ago, although two new marketplaces have since launched; and (4) a senior trader insists that if a client consents in writing, the firm may trade through better-priced protected orders when executing that client's order.
questions.stem: Regarding finding (1), Wexford's written policies and procedures are silent on best execution. Under IDPC Rules 3120 and 3121, the firm's policies and procedures must:

options.A: Address only the commission charged on each trade
options.B: Defer entirely to each trader's professional judgment
options.C: Guarantee execution at the best displayed price on the listing exchange
options.D: Specifically address achieving best execution for client orders and transactions, including the broad factors of price, speed of execution, certainty of execution, and overall cost when passed on to clients

questions.rationale: IDPC 3120(1) requires the policies and procedures to specifically address achieving best execution for client orders and transactions, and 3121(1) requires that for listed securities, foreign exchange-traded securities and listed derivatives they address the broad factors: the price of the security or derivative, the speed of execution, the certainty of execution, and the overall cost of the transaction when costs are passed on to clients. Commission-only coverage (A) is one slice of cost. Trader discretion alone (B) is the absence of policies. A listing-exchange price guarantee (C) misstates the multi-factor, multi-marketplace framework.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member’s policies and procedures must specifically address achieving best execution for client orders and transactions." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4106

questions.scenario: Maren Holt is the designated trading Supervisor at Tobermory Securities, a small CIRO Dealer Member that executes retail client orders in listed securities across several Canadian marketplaces. During an internal review, Holt finds: (1) the firm's written policies and procedures say nothing specific about how best execution is achieved for client orders; (2) a cost-saving proposal on her desk recommends sending all client orders in listed securities in bulk to a U.S. intermediary for execution without looking at Canadian liquidity sources; (3) the best execution policies and procedures were last reviewed three years ago, although two new marketplaces have since launched; and (4) a senior trader insists that if a client consents in writing, the firm may trade through better-priced protected orders when executing that client's order.
questions.stem: Regarding finding (2), the cost-saving proposal would route all client orders in listed securities in bulk to a U.S. intermediary. Under IDPC Rule 3125, Holt should reject the proposal because a Dealer Member's best execution policies and procedures must not include:

options.A: The practice of sending client orders in listed securities in bulk to a foreign intermediary for execution outside of Canada, without considering other liquidity sources, including liquidity sources within Canada
options.B: Any use of foreign intermediaries under any circumstances
options.C: Routing to more than one marketplace
options.D: Execution of client orders outside regular trading hours

questions.rationale: IDPC 3125(1) prohibits a Dealer Member's best execution policies and procedures from including the practice of sending client orders in listed securities in bulk to a foreign intermediary for execution outside of Canada without considering other liquidity sources, including liquidity sources within Canada. A total ban on foreign intermediaries (B) overstates the rule — routing abroad is permitted with proper consideration and the 3129 disclosures. Multi-marketplace routing (C) is what best execution typically requires, not a prohibition. After-hours execution (D) is not the target of 3125.
questions.source_quote: "sending client orders in listed securities in bulk to a foreign intermediary for execution outside of Canada, without considering other liquidity sources, including liquidity sources within Canada." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4216

questions.scenario: Maren Holt is the designated trading Supervisor at Tobermory Securities, a small CIRO Dealer Member that executes retail client orders in listed securities across several Canadian marketplaces. During an internal review, Holt finds: (1) the firm's written policies and procedures say nothing specific about how best execution is achieved for client orders; (2) a cost-saving proposal on her desk recommends sending all client orders in listed securities in bulk to a U.S. intermediary for execution without looking at Canadian liquidity sources; (3) the best execution policies and procedures were last reviewed three years ago, although two new marketplaces have since launched; and (4) a senior trader insists that if a client consents in writing, the firm may trade through better-priced protected orders when executing that client's order.
questions.stem: Regarding finding (3), the best execution policies and procedures were last reviewed three years ago while two new marketplaces launched. Under IDPC Rule 3126, Wexford was required to review them:

options.A: Only when the Corporation requests a review
options.B: Every five years
options.C: At least annually, and whenever there is a material change to the trading environment or market structure that may impact the firm's ability to achieve best execution
options.D: Each time a client complains about an execution

questions.rationale: IDPC 3126(1) requires review of the best execution policies and procedures at least annually, and whenever there is a material change to the trading environment or market structure that may impact the Dealer Member's ability to achieve best execution — the launch of new marketplaces is exactly such a change, so a three-year gap fails twice. Regulator-prompted review only (A) ignores the affirmative cycle. Five years (B) has no basis. Complaint-driven review (D) is reactive and not the rule's trigger, though deficiencies found must be promptly corrected under 3126(4).
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must review its best execution policies and procedures at least annually, and whenever there is a material change to the trading environment or market structure that may impact a Dealer Member’s ability to achieve best execution for its clients." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4219

questions.scenario: Maren Holt is the designated trading Supervisor at Tobermory Securities, a small CIRO Dealer Member that executes retail client orders in listed securities across several Canadian marketplaces. During an internal review, Holt finds: (1) the firm's written policies and procedures say nothing specific about how best execution is achieved for client orders; (2) a cost-saving proposal on her desk recommends sending all client orders in listed securities in bulk to a U.S. intermediary for execution without looking at Canadian liquidity sources; (3) the best execution policies and procedures were last reviewed three years ago, although two new marketplaces have since launched; and (4) a senior trader insists that if a client consents in writing, the firm may trade through better-priced protected orders when executing that client's order.
questions.stem: Regarding finding (4), the senior trader claims written client consent lets the firm trade through better-priced protected orders. Under IDPC Rule 3128, Holt should correct him because:

options.A: Client consent permits trade-throughs if renewed annually
options.B: Trade-throughs are permitted whenever the client order exceeds $100,000
options.C: Only verbal consent is invalid; written consent suffices
options.D: Despite any instruction or consent of the client, best execution of a client order for a listed security is subject to compliance with the Order Protection Rule under Part 6 of the Trading Rules

questions.rationale: IDPC 3128(1) provides that despite any instruction or consent of the client, best execution of a client order for a listed security is subject to compliance with the Order Protection Rule under Part 6 of the Trading Rules — by the marketplace, or by the Dealer Member where it marks the order as a directed-action order under UMIR 6.2. Annual renewal (A) and written form (C) cannot cure what consent cannot authorize. An order-size carve-out (B) confuses the order exposure exception in UMIR 6.3(1)(f) with order protection, which contains no such consent-based exemption.
questions.source_quote: "Despite any instruction or consent of the client, best execution of a client order for a listed security is subject to compliance with the Order Protection Rule under Part 6 of the Trading Rules" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4246

questions.scenario: Priit Kallas oversees electronic trading supervision at Silverton Securities, a CIRO Participant. Silverton is negotiating to grant direct electronic access to Yarmouth Ridge Minerals' treasury department and to an aggressive proprietary trading firm that is itself registered as a dealer. A third client already trading under direct electronic access runs an automated order system; last month a malfunction caused it to flood one marketplace with orders before staff manually shut it down after twenty minutes of searching for the right procedure. Kallas also learns that this client recently allowed an unrelated company to send orders through its direct electronic access connection, and Silverton has now terminated that client's access.
questions.stem: Silverton is considering granting direct electronic access to the proprietary trading firm that is itself registered as a dealer. Under UMIR 7.13(1)(b), Silverton:

options.A: May not grant direct electronic access, because a Participant shall not grant it if the client is acting and registered as a dealer in accordance with applicable securities legislation
options.B: May grant the access if the dealer signs an enhanced indemnity
options.C: May grant the access provided the dealer trades only its own inventory
options.D: Must first obtain the Market Regulator's exemption for retail clients

questions.rationale: UMIR 7.13(1)(b) prohibits granting direct electronic access if the client is acting and registered as a dealer in accordance with applicable securities legislation — a registered dealer must instead use a routing arrangement. An indemnity (B) or an inventory-only restriction (C) cannot override the categorical prohibition. A retail-client exemption (D) is irrelevant; the counterparty here is a registered dealer, and the route for dealers is the routing-arrangement framework, not DEA.
questions.source_quote: "not grant direct electronic access if the client is acting and registered as a dealer in accordance with applicable securities legislation." — umir.txt ~line 8803

questions.scenario: Priit Kallas oversees electronic trading supervision at Silverton Securities, a CIRO Participant. Silverton is negotiating to grant direct electronic access to Yarmouth Ridge Minerals' treasury department and to an aggressive proprietary trading firm that is itself registered as a dealer. A third client already trading under direct electronic access runs an automated order system; last month a malfunction caused it to flood one marketplace with orders before staff manually shut it down after twenty minutes of searching for the right procedure. Kallas also learns that this client recently allowed an unrelated company to send orders through its direct electronic access connection, and Silverton has now terminated that client's access.
questions.stem: Last month staff needed twenty minutes to shut down the malfunctioning automated order system. Under Policy 7.1 Part 8, what capability must Silverton have with respect to any automated order system used by it or any client?

options.A: The ability to recover all losses from the marketplace operator
options.B: The ability to reverse the resulting trades unilaterally within 24 hours
options.C: The ability to transfer the runaway orders to a jitney Participant
options.D: The ability to immediately override or disable automatically the automated order system, preventing its orders from being entered on any marketplace

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 8 requires each Participant or Access Person to have the ability to immediately override or disable automatically any automated order system and thereby prevent orders generated by it from being entered on any marketplace — a twenty-minute manual scramble fails that standard. Loss recovery from the marketplace (A) is not provided for; the Participant remains responsible for orders including those from a malfunction. Unilateral trade reversal (B) is constrained by Rule 7.11. Re-routing runaway orders to a jitney (C) is not a recognized control.
questions.source_quote: "Each Participant or Access Person must have the ability to immediately override or disable automatically any automated order system" — umir.txt ~line 7011

questions.scenario: Priit Kallas oversees electronic trading supervision at Silverton Securities, a CIRO Participant. Silverton is negotiating to grant direct electronic access to Yarmouth Ridge Minerals' treasury department and to an aggressive proprietary trading firm that is itself registered as a dealer. A third client already trading under direct electronic access runs an automated order system; last month a malfunction caused it to flood one marketplace with orders before staff manually shut it down after twenty minutes of searching for the right procedure. Kallas also learns that this client recently allowed an unrelated company to send orders through its direct electronic access connection, and Silverton has now terminated that client's access.
questions.stem: The existing direct electronic access client allowed an unrelated company to send orders through its connection. Under Policy 7.1 Part 9, Silverton's monitoring of orders entered by direct electronic access clients must be designed to identify whether the client may have, among other things:

options.A: Improperly granted or provided its access under direct electronic access to another person, or engaged in unauthorized trading on behalf of the account of another person
options.B: Achieved returns exceeding the market index
options.C: Used more than one order type in a single session
options.D: Traded on more than three marketplaces in a day

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 9 requires a Participant that has granted direct electronic access to monitor orders to identify whether the client may have breached the Participant's standards or the written agreement, improperly granted or provided its access under direct electronic access or a routing arrangement to another person, engaged in unauthorized trading on behalf of the account of another person, or failed to transmit its client's orders through the proper systems. Outperformance (B), order-type variety (C) and multi-marketplace activity (D) are ordinary trading characteristics, not the breach indicators the Policy enumerates.
questions.source_quote: "improperly granted or provided its access under direct electronic access or a routing arrangement to another person" — umir.txt ~line 7061

questions.scenario: Priit Kallas oversees electronic trading supervision at Silverton Securities, a CIRO Participant. Silverton is negotiating to grant direct electronic access to Yarmouth Ridge Minerals' treasury department and to an aggressive proprietary trading firm that is itself registered as a dealer. A third client already trading under direct electronic access runs an automated order system; last month a malfunction caused it to flood one marketplace with orders before staff manually shut it down after twenty minutes of searching for the right procedure. Kallas also learns that this client recently allowed an unrelated company to send orders through its direct electronic access connection, and Silverton has now terminated that client's access.
questions.stem: Silverton has now terminated the client's direct electronic access. Under UMIR 10.18(2), Silverton must:

options.A: Keep the termination confidential to protect the client relationship
options.B: Report the termination only if the client objects
options.C: Forthwith report to the Market Regulator the fact that it has terminated the client's access under the arrangement for direct electronic access
options.D: Wait for the marketplace to make the report on its behalf

questions.rationale: UMIR 10.18(2) requires a Participant that has provided access through direct electronic access or a routing arrangement to forthwith report to the Market Regulator the fact that it has terminated the access of the client (or investment dealer or foreign dealer equivalent), and likewise to report knowledge or reason to believe the client has or may have breached a material provision of the standards or the written agreement. Confidentiality (A) and client objection (B) cannot displace the gatekeeper duty. Marketplace reporting under 10.18(1) (D) covers the marketplace's own terminations of Participant access, not the Participant's termination of its client.
questions.source_quote: "has terminated the access of the client under the arrangement for direct electronic access" — umir.txt ~line 10744
